speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is March 3, 1997. The survivor is Ursula Levy. The interviewer is Marie Kaufman. And it is in Los-- in Beverly Hills, United States of America. And the interview is in English. Beverly Hills, California, United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Marie Kaufman. I am the interviewer for the Shoah Foundation. And today I'm interviewing Ursula Levy. The date is March 3, 1997. We are in Beverly Hills, California, United States of America. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Good morning, Ursula. I would like for you to start by saying your full name, where you were born, and the date of your birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Ursula Levy. I was born in Osnabruck, Germany. And the date of my birth is May 11, 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you begin by telling me the-- who was the-- who were the members of your family, their names, mother, father, sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father is Max Levy. My mother is Lucia Levy, nee Hope-- and my brother George Levy, and I. That was the immediate family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what memories do you have of before the war, in your growing up with your family? Do you remember any holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember holidays. But I remember-- probably the earliest memory I have is of my father. I must have been 3 and 1/2 at that time. My father was in the hospital. And he had come back from the Sachsenhausen concentration camp. And he had gangrene of the legs as a result of the torture that he endured in Sachsenhausen. He had his face to the wall. And I always remember that, that he didn't turn around to say anything to us or to look at us. And I imagine that perhaps he was in a state of unconsciousness, and that's why he didn't speak with us or turn to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it that he got to Sachsenhausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: He got to Sachsenhausen on the Crystal Night. He was captured, along with my Uncle Ludwig Levy, my father's brother, who lived with us. And both of them were captured on November 11, 1938. And they were placed in Sachsenhausen. And they stayed there for about a month. I have their dismissal papers from Sachsenhausen with the commandant stamp. And my father-- my Uncle Ludwig died in December of 1939. I also have his death certificate. And my father-- I don't have his death certificate-- but he died, I think, in March of 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you know happened to him in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were exposed somehow to very cold temperatures. And both of them came back with injuries to their leg, which developed into gangrene. And then both my father and my Uncle Ludwig died within a few months-- my Uncle Ludwig actually within a few days after his dismissal from Sachsenhausen camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So this was 1939--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was the year What did your father do for a living before all of this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my father had a textile business which had been in the family for generations. My grandfather had the business. He-- we made suits and dresses. And we made the trousseau, embroidered the linen for the trousseau, the tablecloths, the towels. We also made the down comforters. And it was quite a good sized store from what I hear.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother worked with the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I'm not sure about. But I also have some very nice memories of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember sleeping in the bed with my mother-- probably it was after my father died. And I heard a dog barking outside. And I've always been-- as long as I can remember, I've been afraid of dogs-- maybe because of the police dogs that were prevalent in Germany at that time, and that, of course, I always saw in the concentration camps later. But for some reason, I was afraid of dogs. So I started to cry. And I remember my mother put her arm around me. And like a miracle, all of my fears disappeared. And that was such a beautiful memory of her. I also remember all of the songs that she sang. My mother sang a lot. And she sang a song which was popular in Germany in the '30s. It was called, 'Ich tanze mit dir in den Himmel, in den siebenden Himmel der Liebe.' In English, the translation is, 'I danced with you into heaven, the seventh heaven of love.' And this song gives me a picture-- a beautiful picture of heaven-- as if it were a ballroom. I've always loved dancing. So the idea of death and what comes after death is pictured in my mind as if we're going to dance into heaven, and there will be a beautiful ballroom-- because of this song my mother sang.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember many other songs she taught me. Do you know the names of any of it-- of the other ones?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She sang another song that was a hit. That's why I-- I think my mother, you know, taught them to me rather than at a nursery school. Another song was a carnival hit, 'Heidewitzka, Herr Kapitaen', and some children songs, 'War Ein Sein Kleines,' 'Bin Allein Zum, Zum, Zum, Zum', and other children's songs she taught me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I'm not sure about. What my aunt told me was that my mother's side of the family was much more liberal-- not religious at all. My father's side of the family was more religious. I remember eating matzah. I-- I've always loved to eat matzah. And I think it stems from the fact that we ate it at home. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Ursula, you were just telling me about some of your memories of maybe some religious observances that went on, or some holiday observance with matzah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember we ate matzah. But I don't really remember the religious services.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember identifying as Jewish with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not consciously. No, I didn't. Consciously, I didn't, no. I mean, I-- I never thought to myself, well, I am Jewish, or that I had any reason to believe I am Jewish. I know we ate matzah. That that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when Kristallnacht happened, do you have memories of anyone talking about it? Or what--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So tell me what happened to you in-- after Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after Kristallnacht, my mother, of course, was desperate for her two children-- my brother George, who is five years older than I, and myself. So she contacted my uncle Joseph, who was here in America, who was married to my mother's sister. And she wrote them and told them what had happened to both my father and my uncle Ludwig. They had both died. And she feared for the safety of her two young children. And she asked if there was any way that he could help her get the children out of Germany. And he wrote back that, yes, there was a contact in Holland, a Mr. and Mrs. Van Mackelenbergh, who helped Jewish children out of Germany and put them in safe places in Holland. Of course, this was a dreadful decision for my mother to make. It must have been so dreadful for her. On the one hand, you know, she had the children-- to send the children away alone at that young age. But on the other hand, she was happy that there was a possibility to save them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she tell you? Do you have memories of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't. I-- I remember that my brother and I were on the train. I do remember that, because I had to go to the bathroom. And the flushing scared me. So I asked my brother to come to the toilet with me. And he objected to that a little bit, of course, coming-- going to the toilet with me. But I remember being on that train, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much older than--

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother George is five years older than I.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you-- did your parents take you-- did your mother take you to the train station?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I'm not sure about. She must have.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So your memory begins with being on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what was that like? Do-- what was that like for you and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think I didn't realize that I was going to leave my-- my mother for any length of time, until, of course, we were in Holland. And then I cried a lot. I did cry a lot. And when we first came to Holland, we were placed in-- it was like a-- a large home where a lot of children were assembled-- assembled in Rotterdam. It was called Haidplatz. And I remember there were a lot of children there. And we would play there outside. And I did miss my-- my parents a lot. And then from there, Mr. Van Mackelenbergh picked us up one day and drove us to a little town called Eersel, where there was a convent with nuns. And it was a home where-- children who are undernourished-- at that time, food was scarce. Between the two world wars there was just nothing to eat. If people had, you know, bread and butter, they were lucky. And so there were a lot of undernourished children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the government, in-- in conjunction with the Catholic Church or other religious organizations, had these homes where children came for a period of six weeks, mostly to gain weight. Because, you know, to gain weight meant that you were healthy. To be heavy meant you were healthy-- a little different.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we were in this-- in this convent with-- I was with 100 girls. And my brother was with about 100 Dutch boys. The Dutch children would come for a six-week period. And they would eat a lot of good food-- rich food, very fatty-- so they would gain weight. And we would go wandering in the woods every day and breathe fresh air, go to sleep very early, and nap in the afternoon. And-- and that lasted for six weeks for the Dutch children. But among those 200 Dutch children were about 50 Jewish children who were hidden there. Of the 50, there were 45 who were half Jewish. One of the parents was Jewish. The other parent was Catholic. The half Jewish children were not sent to a concentration camp in Holland. Of the 50 children, there were five of us who were Jewish on both sides of the family. My brother and I were two of the five. All five of us were sent to concentration camps. And of the five, only my brother and I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So how long did you stay in this particular convent before going on to the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived there in April of '39. And then I left, because we were sent to a concentration camp in April of '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was four years. And then after the camp, we lived there another two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your memories of the convent in terms of the things you did, who you talked to, games you played?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to-- to nursery school first. And we learned poems. And I still remember those poems. And then we would come home. And we would-- well, first we had to go to mass. That was first. Six o'clock every morning we went to mass. And then we had breakfast. We had to eat two big sandwiches with-- with like salt pork. It was smoked salt pork. We had to eat that for breakfast. That was four slices, or else a big bowl of oatmeal. And then we went to school, which I always liked. I always liked school. And then we went-- we would go back to the convent to eat lunch, usually pea soup or lentil soup. And we could never talk. We were not allowed to talk while we were eating. You know, with 100 children, that was the way they maintained some kind of order. But no, we-- we were not allowed to talk. Then in the afternoon, we would sleep after lunch. And then in the afternoon, we-- we would go to the woods. In that part of Holland, there are some woods, unlike the other parts of Holland. And we would play in the woods. And it was beautiful. The air was so beautiful. And we would wander, and walk, and sing as we-- we went to the woods. And then we would come back. The nuns-- we had a nun, a sister-- Sister Brigitta. She took care of the German Jewish children, because we spoke German. And she was a German nun. And she took care of me. And sometimes I wanted to run away. I told her, I'm not staying here anymore. When I got angry, when somebody tried to dress me, or so-- you know, I wanted to dress myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she would say, well, where are you going? And I'd say, I'm going to my aunt in Amsterdam. And I'm taking my tricycle with me. So she'd gave me a big box. And I would pick my things.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] And that would be about as far as I got. But there was also a-- a priest at the convent who was in charge there, who took a special interest in my brother and me. And he wrote my mother in German-- always. And my mother always wrote to us beautiful picture cards with fairytale scenes of or And he would read them to us. And he asked my mother if he could baptize us. And she wrote a letter to the bishop in 's-Hertogenbosch, which is the-- the largest-- next largest city in that part of Holland. And she gave her permission to have us baptized. And of course, they were very happy to have us baptized, because people of the Catholic faith believe that if you're not baptized, you're going to spend the rest of your life-- your eternal life-- in-- in hell.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Rector Wijers baptized us. But even before he baptized us, he paid special attention to my brother and me. I don't know exactly why, except that I was the youngest child there, and maybe that was the reason.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he would invite us to the rectory to have dinner with him. And that was always a big celebration, because the food was just delicious. The noise-- the nuns spoiled him somewhat. And on Sundays, he would visit one of his relatives in a nearby little town called Valkenswaard. And he would take me along. And during the summer, I spent summer vacations with his brother, Uncle Herman and Aunt Hunda, in Tilburg. And he really-- he treated us like we were his family. There was also a niece of his who lived in the little town of Eersel. The convent was in a little town of Eersel, in Holland. And he would visit there sometimes on Sundays. And he would take me along. And-- and they had a girl, Elsie Raajmakers, who was about my age. And we-- we were very good friends. But he was exceptionally nice to us. And he was really like a father. I considered him a father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell us his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name a Rector Leo Wijers.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how long did you continue to receive letters from your mother? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: That I'm not absolutely sure about-- probably until about 1940, '41, because then she also was sent to a concentration camp, to Riga, in Latvia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So-- so you were in this convent until '43?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until 1943-- then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1940, May of 1940, the day before my fifth birthday-- I remember that distinctly-- it was May 10, 1940. We heard airplanes buzzing overhead. And I thought that was very exciting, you know, not knowing what that meant. But the Germans then captured Holland. They invaded Holland. And they conquered the country in a matter of days. At that time every citizen in the town, or whoever lived in the town, had a registration card with all the identifying information listed in the city hall of every little town in Holland, and I think in Germany also. So whoever came into office knew exactly who lived in the town and why. And after the Germans invaded and conquered Holland, they replaced the people who were in office, the Dutch mayor, the Dutch governors, with their own people. And so they put a Nazi mayor in that little town. And that Nazi mayor, of course, knew that there were Jewish children hidden at the convent. In 1941, already, the nuns, the Mother Superior, Sister Renaldas, who was an exceptionally brave woman-- and she, together with the rector, received notice that they had to hand over Annamarie and Evi Goldberg. They were placed in concentration camps. And they died Auschwitz, already in 1941. The following year, the third-- all Jewish girl-- Ilse Brul-- Ilse was a girl who was very proud to be Jewish. And I don't think that-- she wasn't discriminated against because she wanted to be Jewish. You know, remember, most of the children there were Catholic, because they were half Catholic. We were baptized. But she was 16, 17 at that time. And she said, no, I am Jewish. And I want to remain Jewish. That is my heritage. That is the religion of my parents. And she was a very unusual girl. And she made a tremendous impression on me, even though she was not one of the girls that played with me a lot, because she kind of kept to herself. She was somewhat reserved. But she had a presence and a security about her that impressed me as a child. Well, in 1942, the officers came to the convent. And they-- it was time for Ilse to be sent to a concentration camp. And Ilse was sent to Auschwitz. On the train, she wrote a letter to the Mother Superior of the convent, thanking her. And I have the letter in-- in the book-- that I wrote of my memoirs-- her letters and clothes. She thanked the nuns. She thanked the Mother Superior, Sister Brigitte. And you can see that she's writing on the train, because her handwriting is very shaky. She also wrote a letter to her Aunt Toni. And she said, you know, I am feeling so anxious. But I am going to trust in God. And there are two lovely ladies here who are on the train with me. And shortly thereafter, in September '43, she was killed. She never returned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see her leave? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I did see her leave. And I was very impressed. The reason I remember it so well was because-- of course, we were all gathered in the-- in the hallway there of the convent. And the-- the nuns, you know, kissed her goodbye and wished her the best. And Rector Wijers wanted to give her a blessing. And Ilse said, I am Jewish, Rector. And-- and there was kind of the gasp from the nuns. But I can understand her-- her-- decision. She felt so strongly about her identity. And it wasn't that she was ungrateful or disrespectful in any way. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the following year-- well, in the meantime, the nuns sent me to another place in Nijmegen, another convent. They thought perhaps they could send us away. But it didn't work out. So we came back. They also sent-- sent me for a little bit to this Raajmakers family in Eersel. But that didn't work out either. So we both came back to-- to Eersel, to the convent.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then in April of 1943, the Nazis came to the convent. And they said we had to be deported. And so the-- a chauffeur came and drove us to s'-Hertogenbosch, to Mr. And Mrs. Van Mackelenbergh. And my brother and I, we cried, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, this is only going to be a little while. And I'm going to visit you in the camp. And that made us feel better. And then from there, we went to camp Vught, which is very close to s'-Hertogenbosch.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to ask you to hold the thought, because we're going to switch the tape. And we'll continue.
